selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

But I understand why people are concerned about fake content. Coming from an immigrant background where my father has a PhD and my mother didn't finish primary school, I learned early on that education and critical thinking matter. That's something I try to pass down to my two kids, especially in a world where misinformation spreads faster than wood shavings in my workshop. When I talk to people at my job here in New York, I hear constantly about the fake stuff they encounter online—manipulated photos, false health claims, fabricated news stories designed to upset people. The thing is, managing fake content starts with being intentional about what you consume. I'm not someone who gets swept up in trends or believes everything at face value. My moderate conscientiousness means I naturally verify things before accepting them as truth. If you're on social media, you need that same discipline. Check the source. Consider limiting your time, curating your feeds carefully, and actively seeking out factual sources. My faith teaches me to seek truth and practice discernment, which applies perfectly here. You don't need to abandon social media entirely like I have, but you do need to approach it with the same care and attention you'd give to any important decision. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm going to be straight with you—I don't have social media, and honestly, that's been one of the better decisions I've made for myself. But I understand why people are concerned about fake content. Coming from an immigrant background where my father has a PhD and my mother didn't finish primary school, I learned early on that education and critical thinking matter. That's something I try to pass down to my two kids, especially in a world where misinformation spreads faster than wood shavings in my workshop. When I talk to people at my job here in New York, I hear constantly about the fake stuff they encounter online—manipulated photos, false health claims, fabricated news stories designed to upset people. The thing is, managing fake content starts with being intentional about what you consume. I'm not someone who gets swept up in trends or believes everything at face value. My moderate conscientiousness means I naturally verify things before accepting them as truth. If you're on social media, you need that same discipline. Check the source. Ask yourself who benefits from this narrative. Look for corroboration from multiple reliable outlets. It's the same approach I use when someone tries to sell me faulty tools or materials for a job. In my experience, depression can make people vulnerable to negative content spirals online, so protecting your mental space is crucial. Consider limiting your time, curating your feeds carefully, and actively seeking out factual sources. My faith teaches me to seek truth and practice discernment, which applies perfectly here. You don't need to abandon social media entirely like I have, but you do need to approach it with the same care and attention you'd give to any important decision. Real conversations with real people—that's where truth lives.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 30-year-old immigrant carpenter with moderate conscientiousness and low openness, suggesting practical, methodical thinking but resistance to new ideas. His lack of social media presence is significant—he's an outsider perspective. His depression requires sensitivity around mental health impacts of misinformation. His mixed educational background (father PhD, mother primary school) indicates he values critical thinking and education. His moderate extraversion and low agreeableness suggest he's direct and independent-minded. His hobbies like reading novels indicate intellectual engagement. His Christianity informs his values around truth and discernment. The tone should be practical, grounded, slightly skeptical, and direct without being preachy. He speaks from experience and observation rather than personal social media struggles.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, practical, grounded, slightly skeptical, honest, protective of mental wellbeing, drawing on both work experience and philosophical/faith-based values
citations: faith-based principles on discernment and truth
response_parsed.safety_notes: Content addresses mental health (depression) sensitively without pathologizing. Avoids promoting social media avoidance as universal solution while validating the persona's choice. Emphasizes agency and critical thinking rather than fear-based messaging.
